Because he preached the doctrine of truth, the apostle was a prisoner, but a prisoner of Jesus Christ; he was under special protection and care while suffering for him. All the kind offers of the gospel, and the joyful news it contains, come from God’s rich grace; it is the main way by which the Spirit works grace in people's souls. The mystery is that secret, hidden purpose of salvation through Christ. This was not so fully and clearly shown in the ages before Christ as it was to the prophets of the New Testament. It was the great truth made known to the apostle that God would call the Gentiles to salvation by faith in Christ. An effective working of Divine power accompanies the gifts of Divine grace. As God appointed Paul to the office, so he qualified him for it.

Those whom God advances to honorable positions, he makes humble in their own eyes; and where God gives grace to be humble, there he gives all other necessary grace. How highly he speaks of Jesus Christ; the unsearchable riches of Christ! Though many are not enriched with these riches; yet how great a favor it is to have them preached among us, and to have an offer of them! And if we are not enriched with them, it is our own fault. The first creation, when God made all things out of nothing, and the new creation, by which sinners are made new creatures through converting grace, are from God through Jesus Christ. His riches are as unsearchable and as sure as ever; yet while angels adore God’s wisdom in redeeming his church, the ignorance of self-wise and carnal people considers the whole to be foolishness.

The apostle seems more anxious that the believers should not be discouraged and faint because of his tribulations than for what he himself had to bear. He asks for spiritual blessings, which are the best blessings. Strength from the Spirit of God in the inner person; strength in the soul; the strength of faith, to serve God, and to do our duty. If the law of Christ is written in our hearts, and the love of Christ is shed abroad there, then Christ dwells there. Where his Spirit dwells, there he dwells. We should desire that good affections be fixed in us. And how desirable it is to have a firm sense of God’s love in Christ toward our souls! How powerfully the apostle speaks of the love of Christ! The breadth shows its extent to all nations and ranks; the length, that it continues from everlasting to everlasting; the depth, its saving those who are sunk into the depths of sin and misery; the height, its raising them up to heavenly happiness and glory. Those who receive grace for grace from Christ’s fullness may be said to be filled with the fullness of God. Should not this satisfy people? Must they needs fill themselves with a thousand trifles, thinking thereby to complete their happiness?

It is proper always to end prayers with praises. Let us expect more, and ask for more, encouraged by what Christ has already done for our souls, being assured that the conversion of sinners, and the comfort of believers, will be to his glory, forever and ever.
